title: Love Is A Weapon Of Choice

jokes.text: Bret and Jemaine doing impressions of each other's acquaintances — Bret does 'Do it now!' and Jemaine correctly identifies it as Bret's mom
jokes.setup: Bret is doing an impression and asks Jemaine to guess who it is
jokes.punchline: Jemaine immediately and correctly identifies 'Do it now!' as Bret's mom, implying she's bossy/demanding in a specific, vivid way
jokes.explanation: A nice cold-open bit that establishes the duo's easy rapport. The specificity of 'Do it now!' as the identifying phrase is funny — it implies a very particular maternal energy. Works as character world-building.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Do it now!

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Oh, hello, Bret

jokes.text: Bret does a shriek impression and Jemaine guesses 'Victoria. Victoria Bartosh.' Bret: 'Oh yes, that sounds like her.'
jokes.setup: Bret makes an alarming screaming sound as an impression
jokes.punchline: Jemaine identifies it as a specific named person — Victoria Bartosh — and Bret confirms, implying Victoria's defining characteristic is screaming
jokes.explanation: The specificity of the full name 'Victoria Bartosh' is the key comedic ingredient — it makes the absurd feel real. The implication that Victoria's entire personality is just screaming is efficient and funny. The best of the opening trio.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I've got a cramp

jokes.text: Bret's impression of a dog running causes a cramp, setting up Barbara's entrance
jokes.setup: Bret is doing a dog impression on all fours
jokes.punchline: He gets a genuine cramp from the physical position, breaking the bit with mundane physical reality
jokes.explanation: Functional physical comedy transitioning to the inciting incident. The cramp is a grounded, realistic intrusion into an absurd moment, which fits the show's style.

dialogue.speaker: Barbara
dialogue.line: he can't have gone far.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: On foot?

jokes.text: Barbara asks if they've seen her dog; Jemaine asks 'What kind of dog is it?' then Bret adds 'Just a four-legged kind of...' — Barbara: 'He can't have gone far. On foot?'
jokes.setup: Barbara is clearly distressed about her missing dog; Bret starts to specify it's a four-legged dog
jokes.punchline: Jemaine asks 'On foot?' — as if a dog could have gone far by other means, treated as a genuine question
jokes.explanation: Jemaine's 'On foot?' is a perfect piece of FOTC deadpan — it's technically a logical follow-up to 'he can't have gone far' but absurd when applied to a dog. The show's signature literal/deadpan register perfectly deployed.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Terrible affliction.

jokes.text: Barbara explains the dog ran away because 'we had a disagreement'
jokes.setup: Barbara explains why the dog is missing
jokes.punchline: She describes her relationship with her dog using the language of a human dispute — 'we had a disagreement'
jokes.explanation: Economical absurdism. The word 'disagreement' applied to a dog implies the owner anthropomorphizes her pet to a comical degree. Sets up her character efficiently.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: tell me about that girl that's so serious

jokes.text: Jemaine: 'Terrible affliction.' Bret: 'Poor Charlie.' — in response to hearing about Charlie's canine epilepsy
jokes.setup: Barbara mentions Charlie has canine epilepsy
jokes.punchline: Both men respond with performatively grave sympathy — 'Terrible affliction' / 'Poor Charlie' — as if consoling a friend about a dying relative rather than a dog
jokes.explanation: The excessive solemnity applied to a dog's condition is on-brand for both characters. 'Terrible affliction' especially has a Victorian gravitas that's absurdly out of place. Also plants the 'epileptic dogs' theme that runs through the whole episode.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: it's serious, I'm delirious

jokes.text: Song: Bret and Jemaine sing an R&B/hip-hop duet in real time about the woman they just met, debating in rhyme whose girl she is — including 'she was careless with her dog' as a lyric
jokes.setup: Immediately after Barbara leaves, the duo breaks into an elaborate musical number about falling in love with her
jokes.punchline: The song narrates the meeting that just happened seconds ago in real time, including absurdly specific details like the dog and the epilepsy
jokes.explanation: This is the show's core comedic form at its peak: the musical number as comedic argument. The song is funny on multiple levels — the R&B/hip-hop genre is brilliantly mismatched with two awkward New Zealanders, the lyrics narrate events from literal seconds ago ('just then / right then / there / over there'), and the content (a passionate love song about someone they spoke to for 90 seconds who was looking for a sick dog) is perfectly absurd. The genre parody, character comedy, and absurdist logic all work simultaneously. Genuinely exceptional for a TV musical comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: all I know, dawg, is that she's careless with her dog

jokes.text: In the song: '¶ are you thinking what I'm thinking? ¶ / ¶ no, I'm thinking what I'm thinking ¶ / ¶ so you're not thinking what I'm thinking? ¶ / ¶ no, 'cause you're thinking I'm thinking what you're thinking ¶'
jokes.setup: Mid-song, Jemaine tries to establish a shared realization using the cliché 'are you thinking what I'm thinking?'
jokes.punchline: Bret refuses the formula, then both get tangled in self-referential logic about thinking that escalates into verbal nonsense
jokes.explanation: A precision wordplay/logic joke embedded inside a musical number. The escalating self-referential loop of 'thinking what I'm thinking' is a legitimately clever piece of comedy writing — it subverts the cliché by having Bret refuse to play along, then the attempt to explain causes the exact conceptual confusion it was trying to resolve. Highly quotable, structurally tight, and perfectly in character for both men.

dialogue.line: The escalating 'then/there/when' exchange between Bret and Jemaine

jokes.text: In the song: the description of Barbara — '¶ blueberry tracksuit pants ¶ / ¶ white chocolate skin ¶ / ¶ and socks? ¶ / ¶ that sounds like her ¶'
jokes.setup: The song uses romantic/food metaphors to describe the woman — 'strawberry wine,' 'like strawberry wine'
jokes.punchline: The romantic imagery collapses into 'blueberry tracksuit pants' and 'socks' — mundane details confirmed with equal seriousness as proof of identification
jokes.explanation: The bathos of 'and socks?' confirmed with '¶ that sounds like her ¶' is a beautifully executed comedic deflation. The song builds romantic R&B lyricism ('white chocolate skin') then punctures it with the mundane detail of socks confirmed as a definitive identifying feature. Economy and structure are excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: no, about 23 seconds ago

jokes.text: '¶ hang on a minute, stop the track ¶ / ¶ Eugene, stop the track ¶'
jokes.setup: The song has been flowing as a spontaneous romantic outburst
jokes.punchline: Jemaine breaks the fourth wall of the song itself, calling out to 'Eugene' to stop the backing track — revealing there is a guy named Eugene running their music
jokes.explanation: A lovely meta-joke that deflates the musical number's spontaneous romantic energy by revealing the infrastructure behind it. 'Eugene' suggests they have a specific person managing their backing tracks even during impromptu park songs. Efficiently funny and adds to the show's world.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Eugene, stop the track

jokes.text: The song contains the lyric '¶ do you mean the girl who came up to us when we were running in the park / just now and she was looking for her epileptic dog? ¶' sung in full melodic R&B style
jokes.setup: The song is in a romantic R&B register
jokes.punchline: The lyric is a completely functional, prosaic sentence describing the mundane situation — sung with full melodic commitment as if it's a romantic declaration
jokes.explanation: A pure FOTC joke: taking a prosaic, logistically specific sentence and singing it in an R&B style without irony. The commitment to the genre while the content is utterly mundane is the show's comedic DNA. Including 'epileptic dog' as a lyrical detail while maintaining musical seriousness is inspired.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Are you thinking what I'm thinking?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: No, I'm thinking what I'm thinking

jokes.text: '¶ was her name brahbrah? ¶ / ¶ no, I think it was Barbara ¶ / ¶ her name was brahbrah ¶' — escalating sung argument about Barbara's name
jokes.setup: Mid-song, Jemaine asks if the woman's name was 'brahbrah'
jokes.punchline: Bret insists it's Barbara; Jemaine doubles down; they argue in song until '¶ there's no such name as brahbrah ¶' — establishing the running gag
jokes.explanation: The 'brahbrah/Barbara' debate is one of the episode's best running gags. Jemaine's confident mispronunciation and stubborn insistence on 'brahbrah' — a word that doesn't exist — is perfectly in character. The payoff at the end (Barbara revealing her name IS Brahbrah) makes this a standout callback. The sung version here establishes the joke beautifully.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Brahbrah

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Barbara

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: brahbrah, brahbrah

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Barbara, Barbara

jokes.text: In the song: '¶ she had her eye on my knee ¶ / ¶ dawg, I'm sorry, she had her eye on my guns ¶ / ¶ oh, you're loco, she was checking out my buns ¶' — competitive hip-hop boasting in song
jokes.setup: The song shifts to the two men arguing over who Barbara was interested in
jokes.punchline: They compete using hip-hop boasting conventions, but the body parts they're proud of are comically specific and un-sexy: 'knee,' 'guns,' 'buns'
jokes.explanation: Good parody of competitive hip-hop braggadocio filtered through FOTC's awkward, unself-aware characters. 'She had her eye on my knee' is the standout — such a specific and unromantic body part to brag about.

dialogue.line: 'white chocolate skin' - poetic description of Barbara in song

jokes.text: '¶ wait a minute, you talking about the girl with the lazy eye? ¶ / ¶ I think she might have had a slightly lazy eye ¶'
jokes.setup: Both men have been arguing that Barbara was looking specifically at them — 'she had an eye on me'
jokes.punchline: The revelation that she has a lazy eye reframes the entire argument — she appeared to be looking at both of them because her eye wandered, not because of romantic interest in either
jokes.explanation: Structurally near-perfect. The setup runs for almost two minutes of song (who was she looking at?) and the punchline recontextualizes everything: the answer was neither of them, she has a lazy eye. The misdirection is sophisticated — the idiom 'she had her eye on me' does double duty as both romantic claim and literal description. Exceptional joke construction for a musical number.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: she had her eye on my knee

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: she had her eye on my guns

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: she was checking out my buns

jokes.text: '¶ the girl that's fly with the wonky eye ¶ / ¶ she's smoking with an eye that's broken ¶ / ¶ I think it's hot the way she looks left a lot ¶'
jokes.setup: Having discovered Barbara has a lazy eye, the song pivots
jokes.punchline: Instead of being put off, both men celebrate the lazy eye as an attractive feature — '¶ I think it's hot the way she looks left a lot ¶'
jokes.explanation: 'I think it's hot the way she looks left a lot' is one of the episode's best lines. The pivot from the competitive argument to unified celebration of the lazy eye is charming and absurd. The specific visual of someone perpetually looking left as a romantic attribute is both ridiculous and somehow endearing. 'Wonky eye' and 'an eye that's broken' show good lyrical variation.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: wait a minute, you talking about the girl with the lazy eye?

jokes.text: Bret has rounded up multiple random Yorkshire terriers from strangers to help find Charlie — 'I thought, oh, they might be Charlie. I just got these.'
jokes.setup: Barbara is shown various dogs; none are Charlie
jokes.punchline: Bret reveals he just took dogs from random people walking them on the street, gathering them because they looked vaguely like Charlie
jokes.explanation: A good absurdist visual gag — the image of Bret collecting a herd of someone else's dogs out of misguided helpfulness is very in character. The logic is internally consistent (he wanted to help, these dogs exist) but the social reality of taking strangers' pets is ignored entirely, which is classic FOTC character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: the girl that's fly with the wonky eye

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: she's smoking with an eye that's broken

jokes.text: Barbara shows a photo of Charlie in a kitten costume — 'No, it was a kitten party. They didn't allow dogs. You dressed your dog as a cat?'
jokes.setup: Barbara explains Charlie is in a kitten costume in the photo; Bret assumes Halloween
jokes.punchline: It was for a 'kitten party' — dogs not allowed — so she dressed her dog as a cat to sneak him in. 'It's a great disguise.' 'Thanks.'
jokes.explanation: Escalating absurdism that builds beautifully. First: a dog in a kitten costume. Why? Because it was a 'kitten party.' Why dress the dog as a cat? 'No dogs allowed.' 'It's a great disguise.' 'Thanks.' The sincerity of Barbara's 'thanks' treating the dog's cat disguise as a genuine compliment on her ingenuity is pitch-perfect. The concept of a 'kitten party' that enforces a strict no-dogs policy is also delightful.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I think it's hot the way she looks left a lot

jokes.text: Murray's notes: 'Jemaine, you had great stage presence. Fantastic. Bret... no stage presence, unfortunately.' Bret: 'The last gig? I didn't do that gig.' Murray: 'Exactly.'
jokes.setup: Murray is giving performance notes on the last gig
jokes.punchline: His criticism of Bret's stage presence is 'no stage presence, unfortunately' — which Bret correctly points out is impossible since he didn't attend; Murray treats this as the explanation
jokes.explanation: This is one of the sharpest jokes in the episode. Murray's management style — giving critical notes on the stage presence of someone who wasn't there — is perfect character comedy. 'Exactly' as his response when Bret points out he wasn't at the gig treats absence as the specific thing he's criticizing, delivered without any irony. High economy, exceptional character integration, structurally clean setup/punchline.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: So I grabbed them

jokes.text: Murray's concern that Bret's jazzercise clashes with gig times, leading to an earnest discussion about whether Bret has already paid for his classes
jokes.setup: Murray is trying to reprimand Bret for missing the gig to attend jazzercise
jokes.punchline: The reprimand dissolves into a genuine, sympathetic discussion about whether Bret paid for five classes and got six — and Murray concedes he has to go to them
jokes.explanation: Murray's inability to maintain managerial authority when distracted by practical concerns is great character comedy. The conversation about jazzercise membership fees is a perfectly mundane tangent that exposes how un-serious a music manager he is. 'These classes sound fun' is a lovely deflation.

dialogue.speaker: Barbara
dialogue.line: He's in a kitten costume. It was for a party.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Halloween?

dialogue.speaker: Barbara
dialogue.line: No, it was a kitten party.

jokes.text: Murray: 'Wow. That time of the year again.' — in response to both band members having song ideas
jokes.setup: Both Bret and Jemaine announce they have song ideas
jokes.punchline: Murray reacts as if this is a rare annual occurrence — implying the band only has song ideas once a year at most
jokes.explanation: Economy is near perfect — one line establishes both the band's chronic creative drought and Murray's exasperation in six words. Good character integration since it plays into the show's ongoing joke about the band's lack of productivity.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: They didn't allow dogs. You dressed your dog as a cat?

dialogue.speaker: Barbara
dialogue.line: Yeah, no dogs allowed, so it was the only way I could sneak him in.

jokes.text: Murray reveals past song ideas: Jemaine's 'forest' idea from two years ago; Bret's 'I wish I was a tree.' Murray: 'Quite similar to the forest idea.' Then Jemaine's 'sailing' idea from last year — 'Still finishing that one.' Murray: 'It's been nearly a year now.' Jemaine: 'Well, I don't know anything about sailing.'
jokes.setup: Murray has a ledger of past song ideas to compare
jokes.punchline: The archive reveals: one idea was 'forest,' the other was 'I wish I was a tree' (basically the same thing), and the sailing song is unfinished after a year because Jemaine 'doesn't know anything about sailing'
jokes.explanation: A multi-layered joke. The forest/tree similarity is obvious but Murray points it out flatly. Jemaine's sailing song unfinished after a year because he doesn't know about sailing reveals that he chose his subject based on genre aspiration rather than experience — and has been stuck ever since. This is deeply in character. Excellent character comedy that escalates with each new revelation.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: great stage presence

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: no stage presence, unfortunately

jokes.text: Jemaine's sailing song: 'I don't know anything about sailing'
jokes.setup: Murray notes Jemaine has had a sailing song idea for nearly a year
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's explanation for why it's not finished: 'I don't know anything about sailing'
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy: maximum information about character in minimum words. Jemaine has been working on a sailing song for a year and has done zero research. The casual delivery makes it funnier — he's not embarrassed by this, just stating a fact. Very tight.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Where were you?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I was at jazzercise.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Jazzercise?

jokes.text: Both Bret and Jemaine independently reveal their new song ideas are both about epileptic dogs
jokes.setup: Murray asks what their new song ideas are, one at a time
jokes.punchline: Both men have independently written songs about 'pepileptic/epileptic dogs' — inspired by the same woman they met an hour ago
jokes.explanation: The structural joke here is that their romantic rivalry has immediately manifested in identical creative output — they're so similarly smitten that they've had the exact same song idea. Murray's flat 'So you're both writing songs about epileptic dogs?' lands perfectly. Also pays off the episode's theme of the two men being interchangeable in their pursuit of Barbara.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Yeah, I paid for five and I got six classes.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: And you've got to go to them?

jokes.text: Murray: 'Well, either way you look at it, it's quite a complicated subject, isn't it?' — about epileptic dogs as a song topic
jokes.setup: Murray is trying to manage the situation where both have the same song idea
jokes.punchline: Rather than addressing the obvious problem (identical ideas, clearly written for the same girl), Murray frames canine epilepsy as 'quite a complicated subject' — taking the medical dimension seriously as the issue
jokes.explanation: Good Murray character comedy — he redirects to the wrong concern with complete sincerity. His suggestion of 'sailing dogs' as a compromise is a nice absurdist addition.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: forest' and Bret's 'I wish I was a tree' as 'quite similar

jokes.text: Murray's argument that a song supporting epileptic dogs would alienate 'pro-aids' fans — 'then you'd end up alienating all those people that are pro-aids'
jokes.setup: Murray warns against mixing music and political messages, using AIDS as an example
jokes.punchline: He warns that an anti-AIDS song would alienate 'all those people that are pro-aids' — treating pro-AIDS as a real constituency to be managed
jokes.explanation: One of the funniest moments in the episode and possibly a series highlight. Murray's music-management logic taken to its absolute, literal extreme: if you take a stance, you alienate the other side. Applied to AIDS, the result is that he implies pro-AIDS fans exist and need to be catered to. His confidence in this position, the deadpan delivery, and the complete lack of self-awareness make it exceptional. The follow-up (office survey for pro-AIDS people) escalates it further. Near-perfect character comedy — this could only be Murray.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: It's a song about... pepileptic dogs.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Epileptic dogs.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Mine's about dogs with epilepsy.

jokes.text: Murray asks Greg to 'do a quick whiz around the office and just see if anyone's pro' — surveying coworkers about AIDS
jokes.setup: Murray has argued that recording an anti-AIDS song would alienate pro-AIDS fans
jokes.punchline: He doubles down by conducting an actual office poll on AIDS stance, treating it as a genuine management task for Greg
jokes.explanation: The escalation of the pro-AIDS joke into an actual survey is a perfect comedic callback and escalation. 'Anybody pro-AIDS?' called across an office is a surreal image. Murray's eventual 'we don't have time to do a proper survey' treating it as a legitimate data-gathering exercise he'd normally do is the cherry on top.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: if you were to record a song that was anti-aids, you'd end up alienating all those people that are pro-aids

jokes.text: Murray: 'Let's not get all bogged down on aids.'
jokes.setup: The AIDS survey tangent has fully derailed the meeting
jokes.punchline: Murray dismisses the AIDS discussion as a time-wasting tangent — treating 'getting bogged down on AIDS' as an ordinary meeting problem like discussing catering
jokes.explanation: Excellent economy — one short line closes the AIDS tangent while also being its own joke. The casualness of 'bogged down on AIDS' as if it's equivalent to getting distracted by office gossip is the final beat of a very strong comic sequence.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: How many people do you know who are pro-aids?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: No one's pro-aids.

jokes.text: Jemaine: 'Well, if Toby ever had that, I'd like to think that I tried my best for him.' — referring to his own dog potentially getting epilepsy as justification for the charity song
jokes.setup: Jemaine is advocating for making the epileptic dogs charity record
jokes.punchline: His justification is a hypothetical about his own dog Toby getting epilepsy — framed as personal moral duty, revealing his motivation is less about charity and more about impressing Barbara while also being genuinely dog-loving
jokes.explanation: Soft character comedy — the mention of Toby the dog adds warmth and makes Jemaine's motivations charmingly mixed. Not a big laugh but a nice character beat.

dialogue.line: Murray sending Greg to survey the office about AIDS opinions

jokes.text: Jemaine calling Barbara 'brahbrah' on the phone while scheduling a dinner date, without realizing Bret has also just called her to schedule dinner at the same time
jokes.setup: Bret has just secured a 7pm dinner with Barbara; we immediately cut to Jemaine calling Barbara for dinner
jokes.punchline: The juxtaposition reveals both men have independently booked dinners with Barbara — the visual cut between the two calls makes clear neither knows about the other's date
jokes.explanation: Classic sitcom cross-cutting structure executed cleanly. The 'brahbrah' callback works as a character identifier for Jemaine. The comedy of errors setup is efficient and sets up the inevitable dinner collision.

dialogue.line: Bret calling Barbara 'brahbrah' and her not correcting him

jokes.text: Barbara shows a photo of 'Charlie's enemy' — a dog called 'Señor' — and says 'He goes by the name of Señor. I'll keep an eye out for him 'cause he could be an important lead.'
jokes.setup: Barbara is sharing Charlie photos at dinner
jokes.punchline: Charlie has a named enemy — Señor — and Jemaine treats this dog as a genuine criminal lead in a missing-dog investigation
jokes.explanation: The concept of a dog having a named nemesis is inherently funny. Barbara's complete sincerity ('he goes by the name of Señor') combined with Jemaine treating this as actionable intelligence ('he could be an important lead') is a nice absurdist double-act. The formal name 'Señor' for a dog enemy implies cross-species gang activity.

dialogue.speaker: Barbara
dialogue.line: No, it was for a gourd party

jokes.text: Bret tells Barbara his song is about 'canine pepilepsy' — and says he hopes 'it raises awareness'
jokes.setup: Bret has written Barbara a song; he tells her it's about canine epilepsy
jokes.punchline: Bret's romantic gesture is framed entirely as a public health awareness campaign for dogs — 'I just hope that it raises awareness' — as if he's a charity spokesperson rather than a man on a date
jokes.explanation: Good running gag payoff — the 'pepilepsy' mispronunciation and the 'raise awareness' framing treat a love song as a charity single. Character-consistent and fits the episode's theme.

dialogue.speaker: Barbara
dialogue.line: He goes by the name of señor

jokes.text: Bret downplaying Jemaine: 'He seems smart too, with his glasses.' 'Oh, you like the glasses?' 'Yeah.' 'Yeah, they make him look smart, but he's not smart. He wears those because he has weak vision.'
jokes.setup: Barbara mentions Jemaine seems smart with his glasses
jokes.punchline: Bret's competitive dismissal confirms the glasses are corrective rather than fashion — but he frames it as an exposure of Jemaine's fake intelligence rather than simply explaining eyewear
jokes.explanation: Bret's pettiness toward Jemaine while on a date is very funny. The specific angle — he's trying to expose Jemaine's glasses as a deception — while the actual fact (he has weak vision) is completely mundane, is a good deadpan joke. The delivery implies Bret genuinely believes glasses = fraudulent intelligence claims.

dialogue.speaker: Barbara
dialogue.line: He seems smart too, with his glasses.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: they make him look smart, but he's not smart. He wears those because he has weak vision.

jokes.text: Jemaine arrives 15 minutes early for his dinner with Barbara, finding Bret on his date — 'Jemaine, you're 15 minutes early.' Jemaine: 'That's okay. I'm fine with... I'm just gonna wait.'
jokes.setup: Bret is on a date with Barbara at a restaurant when Jemaine arrives
jokes.punchline: Jemaine has independently arranged dinner with Barbara at the same restaurant; his '15 minutes early' reveals he has his own reservation — both men have scheduled dates with the same woman at the same place
jokes.explanation: The payoff of the dual phone call setup. Jemaine's 'I'm just gonna wait' while standing at his friend's table on a date with the same woman he's also dating is magnificently awkward. His attempt to be casual about it — 'That's okay. I'm fine with...' — before trailing off is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: He reminds me more of a bearded lady.

jokes.text: Bret: 'I'd just feel... I've got 15 minutes left.' Jemaine: 'Well, we can spend 15 minutes together another time.' — offering Barbara a rain check on the remainder of Bret's date
jokes.setup: Bret is trying to have the remainder of his date in peace
jokes.punchline: Jemaine offers Barbara a 'another time' for the 15 minutes, treating it as a scheduling conflict between two legitimate competing appointments rather than acknowledging he's crashing Bret's date
jokes.explanation: Jemaine's complete obliviousness to the social reality — that he's intruding — and his businesslike scheduling of the remaining 15 minutes is a great character beat. 'We can spend 15 minutes together another time' treats Barbara's evening as a shared calendar item.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: As an angel might say

jokes.text: Jemaine brings Bret flowers after arriving at the restaurant
jokes.setup: Jemaine arrives at Bret's table at the restaurant, where Bret is on a date with Barbara
jokes.punchline: Rather than flowers for Barbara, Jemaine has brought flowers for Bret — or ambiguously for both — the gag being he came with romantic props but they've ended up with his male friend
jokes.explanation: A visual gag that works on the ambiguity — did Jemaine bring flowers for Barbara or for Bret? The fact that Jemaine hands Bret the flowers while intruding on his date is simultaneously romantic gesture, territorial marking, and completely inappropriate. Barbara's 'He seems so sweet' in response adds irony.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: He reminds me more of a bearded lady

jokes.text: Bret describing Jemaine: 'He reminds me more of a bearded lady.' — in response to Barbara saying Jemaine reminds her of a puppy
jokes.setup: Barbara says Jemaine reminds her of a little puppy, particularly his beard — 'it's very kind of manly'
jokes.punchline: Bret immediately counters: 'He reminds me more of a bearded lady' — framing the same feature as a feminizing rather than masculinizing attribute
jokes.explanation: Sharp competitive put-down delivered with flat New Zealand deadpan. The callback to bearded ladies (a specific circus/sideshow reference) applied to Jemaine's manly beard is a good subversion. Bret's jealousy makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: But I'm also organizing a benefit to try and raise some money for these poor prophylactic dogs.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Epileptic.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Epileptic.

jokes.text: Jemaine launches into his pitch about organizing a benefit for 'prophylactic dogs'
jokes.setup: Jemaine is at the table making his case to Barbara about caring for epileptic dogs
jokes.punchline: 'Prophylactic' instead of 'epileptic' — a malapropism that transforms canine neurology into canine contraception
jokes.explanation: A new variation on the episode's running mispronunciation gag. 'Prophylactic' is a funnier wrong word than 'pepileptic' because it's a real word with a very different medical meaning. The escalation from 'pepileptic' to 'prophylactic' shows comic invention. Barbara's correction 'Epileptic.' followed by Jemaine's 'Epileptic. That's... yay.' is a perfect beat.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: Two chicks into the same dude?

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: Two guys into the same girl?

jokes.text: Dave's advice: 'It's never easy when you've got two chicks into the same dude. I'm talking about two guys that are into the same girl. Whoa, what the fuck is that? I have never been there. With me it's always been two or more girls into the same guy.' — attributing to himself the more impressive version
jokes.setup: Bret comes to Dave for advice about two men liking the same woman
jokes.punchline: Dave immediately reframes the situation to the version where he's the desirable party, then acts shocked and confused by the actual situation ('I have never been there') — a pure expression of Dave's ego
jokes.explanation: Dave is a great recurring character and this is a perfect expression of his personality — his inability to engage with anyone else's situation without making it about his own superiority. 'The Dave-atronic 5000' as a self-applied nickname is a nice bonus absurdist detail.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: Well, let's just say derek's no longer with us.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: He moved to arizona.

jokes.text: Dave's story: 'I had the same situation once with my friend Derek. Well, let's just say Derek's no longer with us.' Long pause. 'No no no no. He moved to Arizona.'
jokes.setup: Dave starts to tell a story about resolving a love triangle with his friend Derek, trailing off ominously — 'Let's just say Derek's no longer with us'
jokes.punchline: After a pause that strongly implies Derek is dead (and Dave killed him), he clarifies Derek moved to Arizona — but the damage is done
jokes.explanation: Near-perfect joke construction. The setup mimics a dark confession (implying murder or serious harm), the pause sells the misdirection, then 'He moved to Arizona' is the innocent punchline. But the follow-up 'But I think they moved to different parts. It's one of the biggest states.' adds a layer — implying the separation was complete and final even if not murderous. The structure is exactly right: dark implication → pause → mundane truth → grace note. Highly quotable, exceptional economy.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Seems like an odd time for jazzercise

jokes.text: Dave: 'You were put on this earth to precreate.'
jokes.setup: Dave is giving motivational advice to Bret about pursuing Barbara
jokes.punchline: 'Precreate' instead of 'procreate' — Dave's malapropism delivered with full conviction as cosmic wisdom
jokes.explanation: Another malapropism joke, this time from Dave — consistent with his character as a bombastic but slightly uninformed advisor. 'Precreate' as a portmanteau of 'procreate' and 'recreate' delivered as philosophical wisdom is funny because Dave has no idea he's saying it wrong.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: Mel's fantasy about Bret and Jemaine having a baby together

jokes.text: Bret claims to be going to jazzercise at night — 'Me and a couple other guys. Just you and a couple of guys from jazzercise, huh? Getting together and doing some impromptu jazzercise at night?'
jokes.setup: Bret is sneaking out to see Barbara; he claims it's an impromptu jazzercise class
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's increasingly arch questioning of 'impromptu jazzercise at night with a couple of guys' treats jazzercise as an obvious cover story without explicitly saying so
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the earlier jazzercise bit with added irony. Jemaine's flat repetition of the alibi back at Bret ('Getting together and doing some impromptu jazzercise at night?') as a skeptical echo is a clean deadpan technique.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: Mel's fantasy about 'what a child of you and Jemaine might look like'

jokes.text: Jemaine's glasses missing — 'I had a shower and I left them in the sink.' Bret: 'They're probably in the sink. Have you checked the sink?' — then the scene cuts to Jemaine wearing a fake glued-on beard to disguise himself from Barbara
jokes.setup: Jemaine can't find his glasses after his shower
jokes.punchline: The next scene reveals Jemaine has glued on a fake beard to disguise himself while pursuing Barbara — implying he took his glasses off intentionally as part of the disguise, not actually lost them
jokes.explanation: The glasses/beard connection is a nice structural joke — the missing glasses set up the disguise payoff. The scene transition from 'where are your glasses?' to Jemaine in a fake beard is a good visual misdirection.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I'm developing a vaccine for pepileptic dogs

jokes.text: Mel stops Bret — dressed up to look like Jemaine — and tells him she's imagined what a child of him and Jemaine might look like, and 'it looks something like this in my imagination' [implying she shows a drawing/image]
jokes.setup: Bret is dressed up to look like Jemaine (presumably to compete for Barbara's affections); Mel intercepts him
jokes.punchline: Mel has imagined and visualized what a child of Bret and Jemaine would look like — presenting the image with complete sincerity
jokes.explanation: Mel's stalker-fan character is always funny, but this is a particularly strong manifestation. The specific detail that she has imagined and rendered what their hypothetical child would look like is simultaneously sweet, deeply disturbing, and perfectly in character. 'I cannot count how many times I've laid in bed wondering' — this level of obsessive detail reveals a rich, deranged inner life.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I'm developing a vaccine for pepileptic dogs.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I take it every 30 minutes.

jokes.text: Bret: 'Oh, I don't really want to know that.' — flat rejection of Mel's baby fantasy
jokes.setup: Mel has just revealed she imagines Bret and Jemaine's hypothetical child
jokes.punchline: Bret's response is perfectly measured — not horrified, not angry, just mildly declining to receive the information, as if Mel has offered him unsolicited food
jokes.explanation: Pure FOTC deadpan. Bret's flat 'Oh, I don't really want to know that' is funnier than any stronger reaction would be. The understatement — treating a deeply creepy obsession as mild unwanted information — is exactly right for the show's register.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: But I think that's because of the vaccine

jokes.text: Murray is developing a vaccine for 'pepileptic dogs' — 'I'm sorry, are you a vet or a doctor?' 'No, not really.' 'But I guess I'm quite intelligent.'
jokes.setup: Murray announces he's developing a vaccine for epileptic dogs
jokes.punchline: His qualification: 'No, not really [a vet or doctor]. But I guess I'm quite intelligent.' — treating general intelligence as sufficient medical credentials
jokes.explanation: Murray's self-assessment — 'I guess I'm quite intelligent' as a medical credential delivered without irony — is a great character moment. It reveals both his overconfidence and his earnestness. The 'not really' before the intelligence claim creates a perfect rhythm.

jokes.text: Murray has been taking his own unproven vaccine every 30 minutes — 'I'm showing no symptoms of epilepsy.' Bret: 'But you don't... you never had epilepsy.' 'No, I don't have epilepsy. But I think that's because of the vaccine.'
jokes.setup: Murray reveals he's been self-administering his unproven anti-epilepsy vaccine
jokes.punchline: His proof of its efficacy: he doesn't have epilepsy. Bret notes he never had epilepsy. Murray: 'I think that's because of the vaccine.'
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode. The logical fallacy (post hoc ergo propter hoc — 'I didn't get epilepsy, therefore the vaccine works') delivered by Murray with complete sincerity is philosophically absurd and perfectly constructed. The escalating contradiction — 'you never had epilepsy' / 'I think that's because of the vaccine' — is a precision comedy machine. Murray's unshakeable confidence in his own faulty reasoning is quintessential character comedy. Comparable to the better anchor examples.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Sometimes I grow a beard and sometimes I glue one on when pressed for time

jokes.text: Barbara's bag is too full to take the remaining vaccine — 'Well, take that. There's a little bit left in there. So if you do find him...' 'I can't. My bag is so full.'
jokes.setup: Murray tries to give Barbara the homemade vaccine to administer to Charlie if found
jokes.punchline: Barbara declines not for medical reasons — she just can't fit it in her bag
jokes.explanation: Functional comedy — the reason Barbara refuses is practical rather than medical (the obvious refusal), which is a mild subversion. But not a standout moment.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: What, with your feet? I can't see one.

jokes.text: Jemaine is at the bar in a fake glued-on beard — Barbara: 'I don't remember you having a beard yesterday.' Jemaine: 'Sometimes I grow a beard.' Barbara: 'Looks like it's glued on.' Jemaine: 'Yes, and sometimes I glue one on. When I'm pressed for time.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine is in disguise with a glued-on beard to pursue Barbara without Bret knowing
jokes.punchline: Barbara immediately spots it's a fake; Jemaine admits it but explains: 'When I'm pressed for time.' As if gluing a beard is a normal time-saving grooming shortcut
jokes.explanation: 'When I'm pressed for time' is a spectacular line — it frames fake beard gluing as a practical efficiency measure for busy people, the same register as saying 'I don't always have time to iron.' Jemaine's complete sincerity while wearing an obviously fake beard that he's just admitted is fake and then justifying it as time management is some of the best deadpan in the episode. The economy is perfect: one short phrase reframes beard-gluing as mundane common sense.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: The challenge to a duel over Barbara

jokes.text: Jemaine, alone at the bar in his disguise: 'Excuse me, I'm still waiting on two waters. Thank you.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine is alone at the bar, having failed to woo Barbara, still wearing his failed disguise
jokes.punchline: His response to this romantic disaster is to continue waiting for his water order — two waters, implying he optimistically ordered for both himself and Barbara
jokes.explanation: A perfect small moment. 'Two waters' tells us he ordered for Barbara as well — a tiny romantic gesture that has now become lonely evidence of his failure. His persistence in waiting for a drink order for someone who has left him is both sad and funny. The formality of 'I'm still waiting on two waters. Thank you.' as his response to being stood up is quintessentially Jemaine. Exceptional economy — maximum information per word.

dialogue.speaker: Dog Owner
dialogue.line: That's my dog.

dialogue.speaker: Barbara
dialogue.line: I think you're mistaken.

dialogue.speaker: Dog Owner
dialogue.line: See there on the tag? It says mocha.

jokes.text: Recording session: Murray hears a piano and asks 'What, with your feet? I can't see one.' — responding to 'Bret' playing piano on the recording
jokes.setup: Murray stops the recording session because he can hear a piano
jokes.punchline: When told Bret is playing piano, Murray asks if it's with his feet (since he can't see a piano) — then it becomes clear it's a backing track already recorded
jokes.explanation: A mild absurdist moment — Murray's confusion about the piano is mildly funny but not a standout. The 'with your feet?' line has some character comedy but doesn't land as hard as other Murray moments.

dialogue.line: The dog Charlie being revealed as a different dog named Mocha

jokes.text: Bret and Jemaine fight over the microphone during the recording of the epileptic dogs song
jokes.setup: They are recording their charity single; both want to sing lead vocals
jokes.punchline: Their romantic rivalry over Barbara spills into a physical fight over the microphone during the charity recording session
jokes.explanation: The physical fight during a charity recording is a good situational comedy beat — it undercuts the gravitas of the epileptic dogs cause while playing out the Bret/Jemaine rivalry. Murray: 'This is all professional equipment. If you break it I'm paying for it.' is a good manager-who-cares-about-the-wrong-thing moment.

dialogue.line: send a check in the letter to make a setter feel better

jokes.text: Murray: 'If you break it I'm paying for it.' — worried about equipment during the microphone fight
jokes.setup: Bret and Jemaine are physically fighting over the microphone
jokes.punchline: Murray's response to the fight is pure concern about the equipment bill — not the human conflict or the charity recording
jokes.explanation: Good character beat for Murray — his priorities (equipment liability) over human conflict is consistent with his character. Not exceptional but functionally funny.

dialogue.line: The strobe lights gave all of the dogs seizures

jokes.text: Jemaine challenges Bret to a duel — 'I see no other option. I challenge you to a duel.' Bret: 'Oh no.' Pause. Jemaine: 'I accept.'
jokes.setup: Bret and Jemaine have been fighting over Barbara/microphone
jokes.punchline: Jemaine challenges Bret to a duel, then immediately — after the pause — also accepts the duel on Bret's behalf, taking both roles
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best beats. The duel challenge is already absurd, but Jemaine accepting it for Bret ('I accept.' after Bret's 'Oh no.' with a pause between) is a perfect logic escalation — he issues the challenge and then, impatient or enthusiastic, also accepts it. The economy is exceptional: challenge, reaction, acceptance, all in six words across a beat of silence. The pause is doing real comedic work here.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I thought they were dancing

jokes.text: The duel is resolved via the romantic ballad 'Love Is A Weapon Of Choice' — the men use music as their duel weapons
jokes.setup: Jemaine has challenged Bret to a duel
jokes.punchline: The duel is conducted via a heartfelt musical number — the metaphor (love as weapon) is literalized as the actual duel mechanism; they sing AT each other
jokes.explanation: The concept of a love-song duel — using musical sincerity as competitive weaponry — is the episode's most formally inventive joke. The love-as-weapon metaphor in the song's lyrics is being performed as a literal duel, making the metaphor meta-textual. FOTC at their most conceptually ambitious.

dialogue.speaker: Barbara
dialogue.line: I thought you guys were gay.

jokes.text: Song lyric: '¶ every time you walk in through my door / you're extending this one metaphor / you leave me wounded lying on the floor / I think I've seen this floor before ¶'
jokes.setup: The duel song is extending the love-as-war metaphor through its verses
jokes.punchline: 'You're extending this one metaphor' — a meta acknowledgment within the song that the song is, itself, extending a metaphor. Then 'I think I've seen this floor before' is a non-sequitur that deflates the poetic seriousness
jokes.explanation: 'You're extending this one metaphor' is brilliant meta-comedy — the song acknowledges its own rhetorical strategy mid-performance. Then 'I think I've seen this floor before' is a bathos drop that punctures the moment with mundane observation. Both lines work on different registers: the first is a witty self-aware lyric, the second is pure comedy through deflation. Together they're very strong.

dialogue.speaker: Barbara
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, I thought you guys were gay

jokes.text: Song lyric: '¶ there's a battleship loaded with emotions / crashing onto some lying ocean / and I'm afraid it is just as I feared / this ocean is made of my tears ¶'
jokes.setup: The song escalates its war metaphor further with naval imagery
jokes.punchline: 'This ocean is made of my tears' — a melodramatic revelation that the entire ocean metaphor has been describing crying, delivered with complete musical sincerity
jokes.explanation: 'This ocean is made of my tears' is a gloriously overwrought lyric — the kind of dramatic escalation that reveals the song is both a parody of overwrought love songs and an earnest expression of it. Works well within FOTC's register of sincere absurdism.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: Mel's family portrait showing Bret and Jemaine as a couple with a baby

jokes.text: After the duel song: 'Surrender. It's a draw.' — Murray's verdict on the musical duel
jokes.setup: The duel song concludes dramatically
jokes.punchline: Murray, having watched the entire musical duel, adjudicates: 'It's a draw.' — treating the love-song competition as a formal sporting event he has authority to judge
jokes.explanation: Murray's 'It's a draw' as a judicial verdict on the musical duel is a great single-line beat. It confirms that everyone in this world accepted the duel as a legitimate way to resolve romantic rivalry, and that Murray had the authority to call it. Perfect economy.

dialogue.speaker: Barbara
dialogue.line: my name is brahbrah. B-r-a-h-b-r-a-h.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Told you it was brahbrah.

jokes.text: Barbara returns with 'Charlie' — turns out to be a different dog entirely: wrong color, wrong breed, wrong size — 'Maybe that isn't Charlie. He's a different color.'
jokes.setup: Barbara returns triumphantly with a dog that everyone assumes is Charlie found due to the benefit concert
jokes.punchline: A man arrives claiming it's his dog Mocha; Barbara is confused because Charlie has been missing for six years — the dog was never Charlie
jokes.explanation: The mistaken dog is a good comedic reversal — the benefit 'worked' only to surface someone else's dog. The subsequent escalation ('he's a different color,' 'different breed,' 'different size') suggests everyone was so excited about the result they ignored obvious discrepancies.

dialogue.line: Six years' - how long Charlie has been missing

jokes.text: Dave's vaccine now has toffee in it — 'I've got a new batch of the vaccine. It's got toffee in it.' / 'That doesn't sound very medical.'
jokes.setup: Dave's homemade epileptic dog vaccine was already established as dubious
jokes.punchline: The new batch contains toffee — and Dave has apparently taken it personally, because 'he liked it' (referring to Charlie taking it)
jokes.explanation: The toffee addition to the vaccine is a perfect escalation of the earlier joke. The vaccine has become progressively less medical and more confectionery. 'That doesn't sound very medical.' / 'You liked it.' — Dave's defense is that Charlie enjoyed it, which is the most Daveian possible justification for medical practice.

jokes.text: The dog Barbara found is not Charlie — it's 'Mocha', someone else's dog — 'Maybe that isn't Charlie. He's a different color.'
jokes.setup: Barbara, Bret and Jemaine celebrate finding Charlie
jokes.punchline: A stranger arrives claiming the dog — it's Mocha, and is a different breed, different color, and different size. 'He's a different breed.' / 'Yeah, he's a different breed.' — everyone gradually notices the obvious
jokes.explanation: The gradual dawning realization that the dog is wrong — different color, different breed, different size — all noticed one at a time rather than immediately is a good slow-burn beat. 'He's a real stickler, that guy' about the dog owner whose dog was taken is a nice line from Bret.

jokes.text: Bret and Jemaine competing to comfort Barbara — 'Bret, get out.' / 'Sorry, Jemaine's got his creepy hand on your shoulder.' / 'Thanks. Bret, you're crowding her.'
jokes.setup: Barbara is upset about the wrong dog; Bret and Jemaine both try to comfort her
jokes.punchline: They immediately turn the comforting into competition — calling each other out ('creepy hand,' 'crowding her') while ostensibly consoling Barbara
jokes.explanation: 'Creepy hand on your shoulder' as an accusation while simultaneously reaching for her is good competitive cringe comedy. The two men undermining each other's comfort attempts at the moment of Barbara's distress is character-consistent and funny.

jokes.text: Benefit concert speech: Bret: 'It's great to be here. I'd like to dedicate this song to all the dogs with pepilepsy.' Murray: 'Bret, your speech is boring.' Then Jemaine: 'I love dogs with epilepsy.'
jokes.setup: Bret is making a dedication speech at the benefit concert
jokes.punchline: Murray interrupts to declare the speech boring; Jemaine's counter-dedication 'I love dogs with epilepsy' is somehow even more minimal
jokes.explanation: Murray's blunt management feedback ('your speech is boring') delivered publicly at the benefit is funny. Jemaine's alternative dedication — 'I love dogs with epilepsy' — is both simpler and weirder, functioning as a non-sequitur love declaration that treats the disease as the charming attribute.

jokes.text: 'Bret, get out.' / 'Sorry, Jemaine's got his creepy hand on your shoulder.' / 'Bret, you're crowding her.'
jokes.setup: After the false Charlie moment, both men are trying to comfort Barbara simultaneously
jokes.punchline: They begin narrating and criticizing each other's physical contact with Barbara — 'creepy hand on your shoulder' / 'you're crowding her' — in real time, to her face, as if she isn't there
jokes.explanation: The cringe of two men competing for proximity to a woman by critiquing each other's physical overtures — to the woman's face — is a nice social awkwardness joke. 'Creepy hand on your shoulder' is a good specific detail; 'you're crowding her' implies Bret is too close while he tries to point out Jemaine's creepiness.

jokes.text: The epileptic dogs charity song — 'Think About the Epileptic Dogs' — performed at the benefit, including '¶ somewhere there's a golden retriever / who's having a seizure ¶' and '¶ send a check in the letter / to make a setter feel better ¶' and '¶ come on make a donation / and save a shaky dalmatian ¶'
jokes.setup: The duo performs their charity single at the benefit concert for epileptic dogs
jokes.punchline: The song is written with total sincerity in the style of a charity ballad but the subject (epileptic dogs) and specific lyric content ('shaky dalmatian,' 'make a setter feel better') make it inherently comic; the climax includes an epilepsy-themed remix
jokes.explanation: The payoff of the episode's running theme. The song works as a parody of charity singles ('We Are the World' style) applied to dogs with epilepsy. 'Save a shaky dalmatian' and 'make a setter feel better' show genuine comic wordplay craft — breed-specific rhymes that double as literal descriptions. The 'this is the remix / ep-ilep-ep-ep-leptic' section is an inspired absurdist escalation.

jokes.text: Murray's verdict: 'It was good, wasn't it? Yeah, it was different.'
jokes.setup: The benefit concert has just concluded
jokes.punchline: Murray's assessment of the show — 'it was good... it was different' — is damning with faint and confused praise, implying he has no idea what he witnessed
jokes.explanation: Good Murray character beat — his vague, hedged assessment after a catastrophic event is consistent. 'Different' as the most damning possible faint praise lands well.

jokes.text: Post-concert disaster: 'The strobe lights gave all of the dogs seizures.' 'Not all of them.' 'Well, the epileptic ones.' 'Yeah, all of those ones.' Bret: 'I thought they were dancing.'
jokes.setup: Murray calls the concert 'a tragedy'; Barbara explains what went wrong
jokes.punchline: The strobe lights at a benefit for epileptic dogs triggered seizures in all the epileptic dogs; Bret thought they were dancing
jokes.explanation: The episode's darkest and funniest punchline. Using strobe lights at a benefit for epileptic dogs is a magnificent piece of irony — the charitable event caused the exact harm it was trying to prevent. 'I thought they were dancing' is the perfect capper: Bret watched dogs having seizures and interpreted it as joyful participation. The precision of the joke (not all dogs — just the epileptic ones — just all of those ones) shows careful escalating structure. Dark, perfectly calibrated, highly quotable. One of the episode's best moments.

jokes.text: 'Even the golden doodle?' 'Yes.' 'The shih-tzu was dancing.' 'No, he passed away.' 'Dogs can't dance, Bret.'
jokes.setup: The strobe lights caused seizures; Bret still thinks some of the dogs were dancing
jokes.punchline: Escalation: a shih-tzu died. 'No, he passed away.' Jemaine's flat 'Dogs can't dance, Bret.' closes the escalation
jokes.explanation: A perfect escalation of the previous joke. The shih-tzu dying is a dark escalation; Jemaine's 'Dogs can't dance, Bret' is a devastating deadpan close — it treats 'dogs can't dance' as the primary corrective rather than 'a dog died.' The specificity of breeds (golden doodle, shih-tzu) makes it funnier. 'Dogs can't dance, Bret' is highly quotable and a strong comedy line.

jokes.text: Murray's reveal: 'You see, my two lads here both seem to fancy you.' — exposing both men's feelings to Barbara; then 'On the other hand, if you like me, that's a different story.'
jokes.setup: Murray steps in to resolve the Bret/Jemaine rivalry over Barbara
jokes.punchline: After exposing his clients' feelings, he immediately pivots to promoting himself as an alternative suitor — 'if you like me, that's a different story' — completely undermining his manager role
jokes.explanation: Murray's self-insertion as a third romantic option — immediately after playing the neutral manager — is a perfect character beat. 'If you like me, that's a different story' delivered with complete seriousness while his clients are standing right there is very funny. The character specificity is excellent: only Murray would undermine his own mediation by nominating himself.

jokes.text: Barbara reveals she thought Bret and Jemaine were gay — 'I'm sorry, I thought you guys were gay.'
jokes.setup: Barbara has been spending equal time with both men throughout the episode
jokes.punchline: She explains she spent equal time with both because she assumed they were a couple — 'I thought you guys were gay'
jokes.explanation: A competent sitcom reversal — it recontextualizes Barbara's behavior throughout the episode. Not the most original joke (men-mistaken-for-gay couple is a well-worn trope) but serves its structural purpose well.

jokes.text: Mel arrives with a 'mock-up family portrait' of Bret, Jemaine, and their hypothetical baby — 'coming out of you guys'
jokes.setup: Barbara has just said she thought Bret and Jemaine were gay
jokes.punchline: Mel arrives immediately with a fabricated family portrait showing them as a couple with a baby — perfectly timed confirmation of Barbara's assumption
jokes.explanation: A brilliant callback and structural payoff. Mel's earlier confession that she imagined what their child would look like is realized as a physical prop (the mock-up portrait) that arrives at the exact moment Barbara says she thought they were gay — confirming Barbara's belief while also being its own absurd Mel joke. The timing is the real comedy here: it couldn't be better placed. 'Jemaine's the dad, I guess, and I've given birth to this little baby' as Bret's summary is also funny.

jokes.text: Bret: 'Kind of cute.' — about the portrait of him giving birth to Jemaine's baby
jokes.setup: Mel has shown a fabricated family portrait of Bret and Jemaine as a gay couple with their baby
jokes.punchline: Bret's response to the deeply disturbing image is a mild 'Kind of cute.' — both accepting the image and finding something to compliment in it
jokes.explanation: Pure FOTC deadpan. 'Kind of cute' in response to a portrait of yourself giving birth to your friend's baby is the ideal underreaction. Maximum economy — two words that do everything the joke needs.

jokes.text: Barbara: 'Truthfully, I'm interested in Bret if he's straight.' — looking at Jemaine. Jemaine: 'Yeah, this one.' pointing to Bret
jokes.setup: Barbara is deciding between the two men
jokes.punchline: After all the competing, when Barbara names Bret, Jemaine has to explain which one Bret is — implying Barbara still isn't entirely sure
jokes.explanation: Functional comedy — Jemaine having to identify Bret to Barbara after all this is a mild indignity for both. The implication that after all this time Barbara still needs pointing at Bret is a gentle dismissal of the whole rivalry's stakes.

jokes.text: 'Could Jemaine come?' / 'Maybe another time.' / 'But, Barbara, I thought...' / 'My name is brahbrah.'
jokes.setup: Barbara (brahbrah) is about to go for coffee with Bret; Jemaine tries to invite himself
jokes.punchline: As Jemaine begins to protest, Barbara corrects him: 'My name is brahbrah.' — confirming Jemaine was right all along, shutting down his protest simultaneously
jokes.explanation: The episode's best single-joke payoff. The running gag of Bret insisting 'Barbara' and Jemaine insisting 'brahbrah' is resolved at the worst possible moment for Jemaine — when he's about to be rejected. Barbara correcting him to 'brahbrah' vindictively (from Jemaine's perspective, serendipitously) validates his pronunciation while doing nothing to help his cause. The structural perfection: Jemaine was right about the name, wrong about everything else. Also the economy: 'My name is brahbrah' does three things at once (corrects Bret's version of events, validates Jemaine, rejects Jemaine). Outstanding callback structure.

jokes.text: Brahbrah asks 'So, brahbrah, how long has Charlie been gone?' — Brahbrah: 'Six years.'
jokes.setup: Bret and Brahbrah are heading off together; Bret asks how long Charlie has been missing
jokes.punchline: 'Six years.' — the dog has been missing for six years, making the entire episode's search a futile gesture and reframing Brahbrah's 'disagreement' with the dog as ancient history
jokes.explanation: The episode's final punchline and one of its best. 'Six years.' recontextualizes the entire episode: everyone has been mobilizing to find a dog that went missing six years ago. The charity concert, the songs, the searching, the collected terriers, the fake beard — all in service of finding a dog whose absence is essentially a permanent fixture of Brahbrah's life. It's a perfect dark close that also explains why she had a recent photo of Charlie in a kitten costume — that party was apparently years ago. Two words, maximum impact. Economy score of 10 is warranted.

tmdb_overview: Bret and Jemaine become rivals for the attentions of the same woman, who happens to have an epileptic dog that has gone missing.
